description: One of the most destructive diseases of rice causing lesions on leaves and panicles.
symptoms: Check leaves for diamond-shaped white to gray lesions with dark borders.
Check stem nodes: they turn black and break easily.
Inspect panicle branches: they may rot and grains remain empty.
spread_conditions: High humidity (>90%)
Temperature between 25-28°C
Excessive nitrogen

biological: Spray Pseudomonas fluorescens formulation @ 5g/L
home_remedies: Spray cow urine (10%) mixed with water

prevention: Plant blast-resistant varieties like Swarna
Avoid excessive nitrogen application
Burn infected stubble after harvest to break lifecycle

description: A major wheat disease causing yellow stripes on leaves, stunting growth.
symptoms: Check leaves for yellow, powdery blisters arranged in distinct parallel stripes.
Run fingers over leaves; a yellow-orange powder (spores) will rub off.
Check for premature drying of leaves starting from the tips.
spread_conditions: Cool temperatures (10-20°C) and high humidity or morning dew

biological: Use rust-resistant wheat varieties (e.g., PBW 343)
home_remedies: Early sowing to allow crop to mature before rust peaks

prevention: Plant resistant varieties is the only reliable prevention
Eradicate volunteer wheat hosting the fungus in off-seasons

fertilizers_recommended.name: Balanced NPK
fertilizers_recommended.purpose: Avoid excessive Nitrogen which promotes rust

description: A highly invasive caterpillar pest that rapidly destroys maize whorls and cobs.
symptoms: Inspect the central leaf whorl: look for ragged, elongated holes.
Check for large amounts of granular yellowish-brown frass (excrement) in the whorl.
Look for the caterpillar: it has an inverted 'Y' mark on its head and 4 dark spots in a square on the second-to-last segment.
spread_conditions: Warm weather, spreads rapidly via wind-borne moths

biological: Release Trichogramma egg parasitoids
Apply Neem Seed Kernel Extract (NSKE 5%)
home_remedies: Apply a mixture of sand and lime/ash directly into the whorls to suffocate larvae

prevention: Deep summer ploughing to expose pupae
Intercrop maize with legumes like pigeon pea or beans
Erect bird perches to encourage predatory birds

description: A destructive pest of cotton that eats the seeds and damages the cotton fibers.
symptoms: Check the flowers: they fail to open properly and form a 'rosette' shape.
Inspect developing bolls: look for small entrance holes.
Slice a boll open: presence of pinkish caterpillars inside eating the seeds.
spread_conditions: Continuous cotton cultivation, leftover debris

biological: Install Pheromone traps (Gossyplure) @ 5/acre
Release Trichogramma bactrae
home_remedies: Handpick and bury rosette flowers/damaged bolls

prevention: Strictly terminate the crop after 180 days; do not leave stalks in field
Deep ploughing to destroy hibernating larvae
Grow Bt Cotton varieties

description: Viral disease spread by whiteflies causing severe stunting and leaf curling, stopping fruit yield.
symptoms: Check new upper leaves: they curl upwards, become small, crinkled, and leathery.
Check plant size: severe stunting with bushy appearance due to shortened internodes.
Look under the leaves: presence of tiny white flying insects (whiteflies) when disturbed.
Check flowering: flowers drop off and no new fruits form.
spread_conditions: High whitefly populations in warm, dry weather

biological: Spray Neem oil (5ml/L) to deter whiteflies
Install yellow sticky traps (15-20 per acre)
home_remedies: Uproot and completely burn infected plants to stop virus spread (No cure once infected)

prevention: Use virus-resistant hybrid varieties
Raise seedlings inside a 40-mesh nylon net
Cultivate a barrier crop of tall maize/sorghum around the field

fertilizers_recommended.name: Micronutrient mix
fertilizers_recommended.purpose: Help healthy plants resist vector attacks

description: Highly destructive fungal-like disease that causes rapid rotting of potato leaves and tubers.
symptoms: Inspect leaf edges: look for water-soaked, irregular pale green/brown spots.
Check under the leaf in early morning: look for a white, fuzzy fungal growth ring around the spots.
Check the tubers (potatoes): they show brownish-purple sunken lesions and a dry, granular rot inside.
spread_conditions: Cool, cloudy, and moist weather (15-20°C with >90% RH)

biological: Foliar spray of Trichoderma viride or Bacillus subtilis

chemical.usage: Curative spray if disease is observed

home_remedies: Cut and remove the haulms (stems/leaves) immediately if the field gets heavily infected to save the underground tubers

prevention: Use only certified disease-free seed tubers
Ensure good field drainage so water doesn't stagnate
High earthing up to cover tubers completely with soil

fertilizers_recommended.name: Muriate of Potash
fertilizers_recommended.purpose: Improves tuber skin thickness and disease resistance

name_bn: বেগুনের ডগা ও ফল ছিদ্রকারী পোকা
description: Caterpillar that bores into the tender shoots causing them to droop, and later bores into fruits making them unfit for sale.
symptoms: Check the top growing shoots: they suddenly wilt, droop, and dry up ('dead hearts').
Inspect the fruits: look for round bore holes.
Check around the holes: presence of insect excreta (frass) plugging the holes.
spread_conditions: Warm and humid climates, continuous brinjal farming

biological: Install sex pheromone traps (Lucilure) @ 10-15 per acre to trap male moths
Release Trichogramma chilonis wasps
home_remedies: Manually pluck and destroy drooping shoots and infested fruits every week

prevention: Avoid continuous brinjal cultivation in the same field
Plant coriander or marigold as intercrops to attract beneficial insects

fertilizers_recommended.name: Neem Cake
fertilizers_recommended.purpose: Soil application acts as a systemic deterrent

description: Fungal disease causing ripe chilli fruits to rot and drop, drastically reducing spice quality.
symptoms: Inspect ripe/red fruits: look for circular, sunken lesions with black margins.
Look closely at the spots: they often have concentric rings of black dots (fungal fruiting bodies).
Check the branches: severe infection causes 'die-back', where branches die from the tip downwards.
spread_conditions: Heavy rainfall, high humidity, excessive overhead irrigation

biological: Seed treatment with Pseudomonas fluorescens
home_remedies: Collect and burn diseased fruits and twigs

prevention: Treat seeds with hot water (50°C for 30 mins) before sowing
Use wider plant spacing for air circulation
Harvest fruits immediately upon ripening

fertilizers_recommended.name: Calcium Nitrate
fertilizers_recommended.purpose: Prevents blossom end rot and strengthens fruit walls

description: The most notorious pest of cabbage and cauliflower worldwide, causing severe defoliation.
symptoms: Check the leaves: small pale green caterpillars eating the lower surface.
Look for 'window-pane' damage: caterpillars leave the upper transparent epidermis intact, creating a window effect.
Inspect the cabbage head: severe feeding causes the head to be stunted and unmarketable.
spread_conditions: Dry and hot weather, overuse of chemical pesticides causing resistance

biological: Spray Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) kurstaki formulation
Pheromone traps
home_remedies: Spray garlic-chilli extract to deter moths from laying eggs

prevention: Plant Indian mustard as a trap crop around the cabbage field
Practice crop rotation with non-cruciferous crops (like beans or tomatoes)

fertilizers_recommended.name: Sulphur
fertilizers_recommended.purpose: Cruciferous crops require sulfur for strong flavor and growth

description: The most common disease of mango causing black spots on leaves, blossom blight, and severe fruit set loss.
symptoms: Check new leaves: oval, irregular dark brown to black spots that coalesce.
Inspect flowers/panicles: they develop black spots, dry up, and fall off (Blossom Blight).
Check ripening fruits: dark sunken lesions that cause the fruit to rapidly rot post-harvest.
spread_conditions: High humidity, frequent rains, and heavy dew during flowering and fruit setting

biological: Pre-harvest spray of Pseudomonas fluorescens
home_remedies: After harvest, dip mangoes in hot water (52°C for 10 minutes) before storing

prevention: Prune tree canopy to allow sunlight penetration and drying of leaves
Collect and burn fallen infected leaves and twigs in the orchard

fertilizers_recommended.name: Zinc Sulphate
fertilizers_recommended.purpose: Foliar spray to prevent little leaf and boost immunity

description: A devastating soil-borne fungal disease that enters through roots and blocks the vascular system of the banana plant.
symptoms: Check the oldest (lower) leaves: they turn yellow at the margins and dry up.
Observe the leaf collapsing: leaves wilt and collapse at the petiole, hanging down like a skirt around the trunk.
Cut the pseudostem (trunk): look for reddish-brown or black distinct vascular discoloration rings inside.
causes: Fungus (Fusarium oxysporum - TR4 is highly destructive)
spread_conditions: Infected planting material (suckers), contaminated soil/water, poor drainage

biological: Apply Trichoderma viride enriched farmyard manure in the planting pit
home_remedies: Uproot and completely burn the infected plant, including the underground corm

prevention: Plant disease-free tissue-cultured plantlets
Do not reuse fields that had Panama disease for at least 3-4 years
Ensure proper drainage

fertilizers_recommended.name: Lime
fertilizers_recommended.purpose: Apply to soil to raise pH, which somewhat suppresses the fungus

description: A highly contagious bacterial disease affecting lemon, orange, and lime trees, causing lesions on fruits and leaves.
symptoms: Check the leaves and fruits: look for raised, corky, brownish blister-like lesions.
Hold leaves against the light: lesions have a distinct yellow halo around them.
Check twigs: rough scabby lesions causing dieback in severe cases.
spread_conditions: Wind-driven rain, overhead irrigation, leaf miner insect damage

biological: Control the Citrus Leaf Miner pest (which spreads the bacteria) using Neem oil
home_remedies: Prune and burn infected twigs and fallen leaves before monsoon starts

prevention: Prune trees before the rainy season
Spray copper fungicides prophylactically
Use disease-free nursery stock

fertilizers_recommended.name: Magnesium Sulphate
fertilizers_recommended.purpose: Prevent yellowing of leaves, keeping the plant robust

description: A fatal viral disease spread by aphids that causes mottled leaves and rings on fruits, halting production.
symptoms: Inspect the young leaves: prominent yellow mottling, mosaic patterns, and distortion (shoestring effect).
Check the fruit surface: distinct dark green 'rings' or 'C'-shaped spots.
Check the stem/petioles: water-soaked green streaks.
spread_conditions: Presence of aphids, growing papaya continuously near infected orchards

biological: Spray Neem Oil (5ml/L) to deter aphids from feeding
home_remedies: Rogue (uproot) infected plants immediately upon spotting symptoms to save the rest of the orchard

prevention: Grow papaya under net houses during seedling stage
Grow a border crop of maize or tall sorghum to block flying aphids
Grow PRSV tolerant varieties

fertilizers_recommended.name: Organic Manure + Boron
fertilizers_recommended.purpose: Boron prevents lumpy fruit deformation often mistaken for PRSV

description: A serious fungal disease that causes black, scabby spots on apple leaves and fruits, making them unsellable.
symptoms: Check the leaves in spring: olive-green, somewhat velvety spots appear.
Check the fruits: dark brown to black, corky scabs form on the skin.
Inspect fruit shape: heavily infected fruits become cracked and deformed.
spread_conditions: Fungus overwinters in dead leaves; spores release during spring rains

biological: Application of Trichoderma to fallen leaves in autumn to speed up decomposition

chemical.usage: Foliar spray before and after blossom

home_remedies: Rake up and remove all fallen leaves in autumn; burn or compost them away from the orchard to destroy overwintering fungus

prevention: Plant scab-resistant varieties
Prune trees well to open the canopy for rapid leaf drying after rain
Apply a urea spray to fallen leaves in autumn to accelerate rotting

description: Soil with pH less than 6.0 restricts root growth and makes nutrients like phosphorus unavailable.
symptoms: Stunted crop growth and pale green to yellow leaves.
Poor root development, especially in legumes.
Manganese and aluminum toxicity symptoms like burnt leaf tips.
causes: Continuous use of acid-forming fertilizers (like Urea)
spread_conditions: High rainfall areas, poor soil management

biological: Include deep-rooted cover crops to cycle nutrients
home_remedies: Incorporate wood ash or crushed eggshells into small farm beds

prevention: Regular soil testing
Use balanced fertilizers instead of relying only on Urea

description: A critical micronutrient deficiency causing stunted growth and poor yields.
symptoms: Interveinal chlorosis (yellowing between veins) on new leaves.
In rice, leaves develop brown blotches and streaks (Khaira disease).
Shortened internodes leading to rosetting.
spread_conditions: Intensive cropping without micronutrient replenishment

biological: Use Zinc-solubilizing bacteria (ZSB) biofertilizers
home_remedies: Apply well-decomposed Farm Yard Manure (FYM)

prevention: Apply Zinc Sulphate as a basal dose

description: Accumulation of water-soluble salts in the soil root zone, severely limiting water uptake.
symptoms: White crust on the soil surface when dry.
Plants appear wilted even when soil is moist.
Marginal leaf burn.
spread_conditions: Arid and semi-arid regions, coastal areas

biological: Grow salt-tolerant crops like barley or cotton initially
home_remedies: Heavy irrigation to leach salts below the root zone

prevention: Improve field drainage infrastructure

description: A destructive post-harvest fungal disease that rots onion bulbs during storage.
symptoms: Softening and browning of the neck tissues of the bulb.
A gray, fuzzy fungal growth develops on the affected scales.
causes: Fungus infecting through the cut neck during harvest
spread_conditions: High humidity in storage
Improper curing

biological: Ensure thorough drying (curing) of onions before storage
home_remedies: Store in well-ventilated, cool, and dry areas

prevention: Allow onion tops to dry naturally and fall over before harvesting

description: Fungal infection that enters through harvest wounds, causing potatoes to shrivel and rot.
symptoms: Sunken, wrinkled, brown-to-black lesions on the potato surface.
Internal tissue turns brown, dry, and powdery.
causes: Fungus entering through bruises during harvest
spread_conditions: Wounds on tubers, warm and dry storage initially

biological: Maintain strict hygiene in storage facilities
home_remedies: Cure potatoes at 10-15°C with high humidity for 1-2 weeks before cold storage

prevention: Handle tubers gently to minimize bruising

description: Tiny beetles that bore into stored grains, eating them from the inside out.
symptoms: Presence of small, dark beetles walking among grains.
Grains have small, perfectly round exit holes.
Accumulation of fine, powdery dust.
spread_conditions: Warm temperatures and high grain moisture

biological: Store grains in hermetically sealed bags
home_remedies: Mix dried neem leaves or ash with grains before storing
Sun-dry grains thoroughly

prevention: Thoroughly clean storage bins before putting in new grain

description: Common fungal disease causing severe defoliation and yield loss.
symptoms: Small, dark circular spots appear on leaves.
Severe infection causes complete defoliation before harvest.
spread_conditions: High humidity, prolonged leaf wetness

biological: Seed treatment with Trichoderma viride
home_remedies: Spray Neem Seed Kernel Extract (NSKE 5%)

prevention: Crop rotation with non-legume crops

description: Considered the 'cancer of sugarcane', this fungal disease rots the cane from the inside.
symptoms: The third or fourth leaf from the top starts yellowing.
When cane is split, internal tissue is reddened with white patches.
A sour, alcoholic odor is emitted.
spread_conditions: Infected setts, waterlogging, monsoon season

biological: Sett treatment with Pseudomonas fluorescens
home_remedies: Uproot and burn infected clumps immediately

prevention: Use rigorously selected, disease-free setts

description: Devastating disease globally causing severe defoliation and yield loss.
symptoms: Orange powdery lesions on the underside of leaves.
Yellow spots on the upper leaf surface.
Premature leaf drop.
spread_conditions: Rain splash, high humidity, temperatures between 15-25°C

biological: Use highly resistant coffee varieties
home_remedies: Adequate spacing to improve air circulation

prevention: Proper pruning and shade management

description: Tiny, sap-sucking insects that weaken plants and transmit viral diseases like Leaf Curl.
symptoms: Clouds of tiny white insects fly up when shaken.
Leaves become heavily coated with sticky honeydew.
Black sooty mold grows on the honeydew.
spread_conditions: Warm and dry weather

biological: Install yellow sticky traps (15-20 per acre)
home_remedies: Spray Neem Oil (5ml/L + 1ml liquid soap)

prevention: Eradicate weed hosts around the field

description: Caterpillars bore into the rice stem, cutting off nutrient flow.
symptoms: Vegetative stage: Central shoot dries up ('Dead Heart').
Reproductive stage: Emerging panicles are white and empty ('Whitehead').
spread_conditions: Continuous flooded conditions

biological: Install pheromone traps
Release Trichogramma japonicum
home_remedies: Clip seedling tips before transplanting

prevention: Plough under rice stubble after harvest

description: Small insects clustering on tender shoots, sucking sap and transmitting viruses.
symptoms: Clusters of tiny insects on new shoots.
Leaves curl, pucker, and turn yellow.
spread_conditions: Cool to warm weather, high nitrogen

biological: Encourage Ladybird beetles and Lacewings
home_remedies: Garlic and soap water spray

prevention: Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilizers

description: Voracious, swarming grasshoppers capable of destroying entire fields in hours.
symptoms: Massive clouds of flying insects darkening the sky.
Rapid and complete defoliation of plants down to the bare stems.
spread_conditions: Wind currents, arid weather conditions following heavy rains

biological: Use entomopathogenic fungi like Metarhizium acridum
home_remedies: Create loud noises using drums or utensils to prevent swarms from settling

prevention: Monitor swarm movements via FAO bulletins and coordinate early with local agriculture departments

description: The most common nutrient deficiency, causing poor growth and pale yellow crops globally.
symptoms: Yellowing (chlorosis) starts from the older, lower leaves.
Stunted growth and thin, spindly stems.
Reduced tillering in cereals and small fruit sizes.
causes: Heavy rains leaching nitrate from soil
spread_conditions: Sandy soils, high rainfall, continuous cropping without legumes

biological: Inoculate legumes with Rhizobium bacteria
Azotobacter biofertilizer
home_remedies: Apply a thick layer of nitrogen-rich compost or compost tea

prevention: Incorporate green manure (like Dhaincha) before planting main crop

description: Lack of iron causes new leaves to turn yellow while veins remain green.
symptoms: Distinct interveinal yellowing completely restricted to the youngest upper leaves.
In severe cases, the entire leaf turns white or papery.
Shoot dieback if uncorrected.
causes: High soil pH (calcareous soils) making iron unavailable
spread_conditions: Alkaline soils and poor aeration due to waterlogging

biological: Apply heavy organic compost to naturally chelate iron
home_remedies: Burying rusty iron nails (slow and inefficient, but traditionally used)

prevention: Avoid excessive liming
Manage soil moisture efficiently

description: Rapid post-harvest rotting where lesions appear as sunken V-shaped cone of decay.
symptoms: Light brown to dark brown sunken circular spots on the fruit.
Pink or salmon-colored spore masses form in the center of the spots in humid storage.
Internal flesh rot is V-shaped, pointing toward the core.
causes: Fungal infection taking hold during warm storage
spread_conditions: Warm (<30C) and highly humid storage

biological: Store fruits at near-freezing temperatures immediately after harvest
home_remedies: Remove and discard infected fruits immediately as they spread spores rapidly in storage bins

prevention: Rapid cooling of fruits post-harvest
Remove mummified fruits from orchard

description: Moth whose larvae bore entirely inside the grain kernel during storage.
symptoms: Small yellowish-brown moths flying around grain bins.
Round exit holes covered by a thin translucent 'window' cap before the adult emerges.
Kernels feel light and hollow.
spread_conditions: Warm grain storages, storing uncovered crops

biological: Store grains in PICS hermetic bags

chemical.dosage: Used only on storage walls, not directly on food grain

home_remedies: Sun-dry crops to bring grain moisture below 10% before binning

prevention: Use insect-proof grain silos

description: Severe soil-borne disease causing complete drying of the pigeon pea plant at flowering/podding stage.
symptoms: Gradual yellowing and drying of leaves starting from the bottom.
Wilted leaves remain attached to the plant.
Splitting the main stem base reveals a dark black/purple band in the wood.
spread_conditions: Consecutive pigeonpea cropping, surviving years in soil

biological: Soil application of Trichoderma viride enriched farmyard manure
home_remedies: Remove and burn wilted plants immediately

prevention: Grow wilt-resistant varieties like Asha or Maruti
Crop rotation with sorghum or maize for 3-4 years

description: Highly destructive caterpillar that bores its head into pods leaving half its body outside.
symptoms: Round holes in the chickpea pods.
Green to brown caterpillars actively feeding on the seeds inside.
Significant flower and square drop.
spread_conditions: Warm weather, multiple generations per year

biological: Install Heli-lure pheromone traps
Spray HaNPV virus at evening time
home_remedies: Erect bird perches (T-shaped sticks) in the field to invite predatory birds

prevention: Intercrop with coriander to attract natural parasitoids

description: Agreesive foliar disease causing rapid premature defoliation and massive yield losses.
symptoms: Tiny brown or brick-red spots on the upper leaf surface.
Underside of leaves shows raised, volcano-like pustules leaking yellowish spores.
Severe yellowing followed by rapid leaf drop.
spread_conditions: Prolonged leaf wetness, canopy closure shading the lower leaves

biological: No highly effective natural controls once widespread; use early warning systems
home_remedies: Increase row spacing to allow better airflow and leaf drying

prevention: Follow local rust forecasting networks
Plant early to avoid peak rust season
